texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure and mixed gas permeation  experiments  for  olefins and paraffins of  C  2 and C  3 were carried out  for  several polyimide and  other polymer  membranes at  pressures up to  8 atm and  temperatures from 308 to  423 K. The olefins were more permeable  to  the  corresponding paraffins due  to  their preferential diffusion based  on  the  difference in  their molecular size. Polyimide prepared from  4,4'-(hexafluoroisopropylidene)diphthalic anhydride (6FDA) and 2,4,6-trimethyl-1,3-phenylenediamine (TrMPD) displayed relatively high  performance: permeability coefficient to  propylene, PC3H6  =  20-40  Barrer (1 Barrer = 1  × 10-10  cm3(STP)/(cm  s  cmHg)) and  ideal separation factor (permeability ratio of  pure  propylene and  propane). Oqd(C3H6/C3H  8)  =  11 at  323 K and  2  atm.  Polyimide from  6FDA  and  dimethyl-3,7-diaminodiphenylthiophene-5,5-dioxide (DDBT)  displayed low  permeability and  high  permselectivity: Pc3~6  = 0.8  Barrer and Otid(C3H6/C3H 8)  --27 at  323 K and 2 atm.  Their  performance was much better than  that of  other polymers  such  as  poly(2,6-dimethyl-l,4-phenyleneoxide). For mixed gas  permeation, the  separation factor was lower  by about  40%  than  the  aid  due  to  the  increase in PC3H8 caused  by coexisting propylene.

The olefins were more permeable  to  the  corresponding paraffins due  to  their preferential diffusion based  on  the  difference in  their molecular size. Polyimide prepared from  4,4'-(hexafluoroisopropylidene)diphthalic anhydride (6FDA) and 2,4,6-trimethyl-1,3-phenylenediamine (TrMPD) displayed relatively high  performance: permeability coefficient to  propylene, PC3H6  =  20-40  Barrer (1 Barrer = 1  × 10-10  cm3(STP)/(cm  s  cmHg)) and  ideal separation factor (permeability ratio of  pure  propylene and  propane). Oqd(C3H6/C3H  8)  =  11 at  323 K and  2  atm.  Polyimide from  6FDA  and  dimethyl-3,7-diaminodiphenylthiophene-5,5-dioxide (DDBT)  displayed low  permeability and  high  permselectivity: Pc3~6  = 0.8  Barrer and Otid(C3H6/C3H 8)  --27 at  323 K and 2 atm.  Their  performance was much better than  that of  other polymers  such  as  poly(2,6-dimethyl-l,4-phenyleneoxide). For mixed gas  permeation, the  separation factor was lower  by about  40%  than  the  aid  due  to  the  increase in PC3H8 caused  by coexisting propylene.

texts.orig: Separation of  olefin and paraffin gases  is  one of the  most  important processes in  petrochemical industries and currently performed  by the  low-temperature distillation with large  energy  consumption.  Alternative  energy-saving separation technologies should  be applied  to this  process.  Membrane separation has potential to impact it  because of low energy consumption, compact  unit, and simple  operation. texts.orig: On  the other  hand, as for the olefin/paraffin separation through  solid polymeric  membranes without  carrier, only a few studies have been reported. Lee  and  Hwang have  reported  that  a  polyimide hollow  fiber membrane based  on 3,3',4,4'-biphenyltetracarboxylic dianhydride (BPDA)  displayed an  ideal separation factor (permeability ratio of  pure  gases) of 15 for  propylene  (C3H  6) over propane  (C3H 8) [5].

texts.orig: Many polyimides  have more  excellent performance for  H2/CH 4 and CO2/CH  4 separations than other  polymers including  PPO [8].  In this  study, permeability and  solubility coefficients to C2H4, C2H 6, C3H 6 and C3H 8 for  some polyimides  and other polymers  such  as  PPO  were  measured  to  investigate their permeation  and separation properties to olefin/paraffin gases.
texts.text: Many polyimides  have more  excellent performance for  H2/CH 4 and CO2/CH  4 separations than other  polymers including  PPO [8].  In this  study, permeability and  solubility coefficients to C2H4, C2H 6, C3H 6 and C3H 8 for  some polyimides  and other polymers  such  as  PPO  were  measured  to  investigate their permeation  and separation properties to olefin/paraffin gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 1 shows  the chemical  structures of polyimides  and other  polymers  investigated here.  Polyimide membranes  were prepared  as described  in elsewhere [9-11]. BPDA and  4,4'-(hexafluoroisopropylidene) diphthalic anhydride  (6FDA)  were used as acid anhydrides.  2,3,5,6-Tetramethyl-l,4phenylenediamine (TeMPD), 2,4,6-trimethyl-l,3phenylenediamine (TrMPD), 4,4'-oxydianiline (ODA), and dimethyl-3,7-diaminodiphenylthiophene-5,5-dioxide (DDBT)  were used as diamines. DDBT is  a mixture  of  isomers  having  two methyl groups  bonded at  different positions of  the  aromatic rings, namely 63%  at  2,8-, 33%  at  2,6-, and 4%  at 4,6-positions, and was supplied from Ube Industries Ltd., PPO  membranes were prepared by casting the chloroform solution of  PPO  (from  Aldrich  Chemical Company  Inc.) onto  glass plates and then  by drying at  373 K for  10  h in  vacuo.  1,2-Polybutadiene (12PB) supplied from  Japan  Synthetic Rubber  Ltd.  was purified by  repeated reprecipitations (toluene/methanol). 12PB  membranes were cast  from its benzene solution  onto  glass plates and dried  at  323 K  for  20 h in vacuo.  The additive-free membranes of  polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)  and poly(4-methylpentene-l-co~-olefin)  (P4MP) were  supplied from  Shinetsu Chemical Industries Ltd.  and Mitsui  Petrochemical Industries Ltd., respectively.

texts.orig: The characterization f the  membranes were carried  out  as described elsewhere  [9-11]. A  vacuum time-lag method was used to  measure permeability coefficient P, which was determined  from a steady state flow  rate  in  a period  between 5 and 10 times the  time  lag  unless  otherwise noted.  A  sorption cell equipped  with a Sartorius S3D-P  model electronic microbalance was used  to  measure  sorption amount. Apparent  solubility coefficient S was calculated as the  ratio of  the  sorption amount to  the  equilibrium pressure. These  measurements  were  carried out  at  the pressures up to  8 atm and in  temperature range  from 308 to  423 K.
texts.text: The characterization f the  membranes were carried  out  as described elsewhere  [9-11]. A  vacuum time-lag method was used to  measure permeability coefficient P, which was determined  from a steady state flow  rate  in  a period  between 5 and 10 times the  time  lag  unless  otherwise noted.  A  sorption cell equipped  with a Sartorius S3D-P  model electronic microbalance was used  to  measure  sorption amount. Apparent  solubility coefficient S was calculated as the  ratio of  the  sorption amount to  the  equilibrium pressure. These  measurements  were  carried out  at  the pressures up to  8 atm and in  temperature range  from 308 to  423 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Average  diffusion coefficient D  was evaluated by equation  D  = P/S. Ideal  separation factor cqd(i/j), which was calculated as  the  P  ratio of  pure  gases  i and j,  is  expressed by the  product  of  the  D  and S ratios of the  pure gases,  i.e. diffusivity selectivity and solubility selectivity.

The measurement  for each  gas  was carried out  in  order  of increasing  experimental  pressure  for the as-cast membranes.  The data  at  the  pressures less than  5 atm were analyzed  by Eq. (4)  using  a nonlinear leastsquares regression technique, and the  values of  sorption  parameters thus  obtained are  listed in  Table  2. The sorption amounts at  the  pressures above 6 arm were  larger than  those  calculated from  Eq.  (4). Similar deviation from  Eq.  (5) has  been  observed for  CO 2 sorption isotherms  for  other  glassy  polymers  with relatively low Tg [16,17]  and has  been attributed to transition into  the  rubbery  state induced  by sorbed molecules. Adopting ou as d,  plots  of log D versus  d  for  6FDA-TrMPD polyimide  and PPO  are  shown in  Fig. 5.  There  was a  linear correlation between  log  D  and d(  = ou) for  each  polymer. For  6FDA-TrMPD, the  D values  of  methane  and propane  are  3-4  times  larger than  those  of  ethane  and n-butane, respectively, in spite  of  the same  Okt  values  (0.38  and 0.43 nm, respectively). The O'kt is useful in considering whether  a  penetrant molecule  can  enter into rigid and cylindrical pores  like  those  in zeolite; it  is  determined  only by the minimum cross-section of the penetrant molecule, but  not  by the  molecular length. However, the  situation is  different for  penetrant diffusion  in  polymer  matrix, where penetrants diffuse through  free volume  holes  by  co-operation with  local molecular motion.  It depends  not  only  on the  minimum cross-section but also  on the  effective length whether  penetrant molecules  can pass  through  free

The o-kt  is  evaluated as  the  longer  diameter of the  minimum  cross-section and therefore, is  larger for  an olefin than  for  the  corresponding n-paraffin because  of  the  larger ratio of  the  longer  diameter to shorter one of  the  minimum  cross-section. texts.orig: Fig.  7 shows the  plots of Otid(C3H6//c3n8 ) versus PC3H6  at  2 atm and different  emperatures. For BPDA-ODA polyimides, P  was not  determined precisely because  of very low values  less  than 0.05 Barrer  at  373 K. Activation energies for  permeation and diffusion A Ep  and A ED, respectively, and sorption  enthalpy  A H s  for  propylene  and propane in polyimides  are listed in Table 5. The  polyimides investigated have similar values  of A  H s,  which are close  to  the  enthalpies of  condensation of  propylene and propane (-15.6  and  -16.9 kJ/mol,  respectively, at  323 K). The differences in AE  v between both  gases  and among  the  polyimides are  attributed mainly  to  those  in A  Eo. 6FDA-TrMPD and 6FDATeMPD polyimides have much  smaller temperature dependence  of  PC3H6 and  aid(C3H6/C3H 8)  than  the other  polyimides. The 6FDA-based polyimides have higher  P  and smaller A Ep  than  BPDA-based  ones because  of  larger V  F.  For  polyimides with  the  same acid anhydride  moieties  Pc3H6  is  in the order  of TeMPD = TrMPD  > DDBT > ODA and A Ep is  reverse  as  observed for  other gases  [9-11]. This  is  the result from V  v and size  distribution of free  space holes. The polyimides from  TeMPD or  TrMPD  have larger V  F and  larger average  size of  free space  holes than  the  ones from other  diamines  [10].  The  P  is much larger for the  DDBT-based polyimides than  for the  ODA-based  ones and A E  v is  reverse, although V  v is a  little larger. This  also seems  due to  the  larger fraction of  free  space  holes  large  enough for  penetrant molecules  to  pass  through.  These seem to  be due to  their rigid and nonplanar  structure caused  by the  presence of  two methyl  side groups  on the ortho positions to  an  imide  ring.

texts.orig: The rubbery  polymers  such as  P4MP,  12PB  and PDMS have  very  large PC3Hr,  but  very  low permselectivity. Among  the  polyimides investigated in  this study,  6FDA-TrMPD and 6FDA-TeMPD have relatively high performance  of Pc3n6 = 20-40 Barrer and  O~id(C3H6/C3H  8)  =  8-11 at  323 K. PPO  and its  copolymers  with 2,6-diphenylphenol have been reported to  have  much higher performance  of  PC3H6 =  l0 and 2-4 Barrer, respectively, and ce(C3H6/C3H8 )= 18  and  25-69  at 323  K [7]. However,  such  higher performance was not  observed for  PPO  in  the  present study  (see  Table  7).  Permeability coefficients to  CO 2 and CH  4 for  the  present PPO  membranes at  308 K, 48 and 2.9  Barrer, were close  to the values  reported by Maeda  et al.  [28]

texts.orig: Recently, Ilinitch et  al. reported that the  pressure dependence of PC3H6  for  glassy  polymers  such as PPO  and  poly(amide imide)  exhibited a sharp  multifold  rise at  ca.  4.5  atm [29]. They called this behavior  "permeability crisis". The "crisis" increase of Pc~n6 did  not  occur  instantaneously, but  prolonged (3-6 h  for  PPO)  exposure  to a high pressure  of propylene was necessary for  the  membranes to  reach the  new steady state of  high  permeability [29]. In  the present  study,  we  also  carried out the long term experiments. However, the  behavior of  "permeability crisis" was not  observed for  6FDA-TrMPD polyimide  membranes.  For  6FDA-TrMPD (20 txm thick) PC~H6  was increased only  40%  in  10 h at  323 K  and 6 atm.  No  appreciable changes  in PC3H~  and ce for 6FDA-TrMPD were observed  for  mixed gas  (50/50 mol%) permeation at  323 K  and  a  total pressure of  6 atm during  the  experimental period  of  33 h.

Olefins  was more permeable  than  Paraffins due to  their preferential diffusion based  on the  differences in  their molecular  size.  6FDA-TrMPD and 6FDA-TeMPD polyimides displayed  relatively high performance of Pc~H6 = 20-40  Barrer and Oqd(C3H6/C3H  8)  = 8-11 at  323 K  and 2 atm. 6FDA-DDBT polyimide  displayed  low permeability and high permselectivity: Pc3H6 = 0.8 Barrer  and  aid(C3H6/C3H8 )  = 27  at 323 K  and 2 atm.  The 6FDA-based polyimides were much  superior to the  BPDA-based  polyimides, because  of  the  much  lower  permeability for  the  latter. They were much  superior to PPO  and the  rubbery polymers  such  as  P4MP.
